section.title: Samson's Revenge
section.sectionRationale: The lost bride sets off a chain of escalating revenge. Samson burns the Philistine harvest with foxes and firebrands; they burn his wife and her father; he strikes them in slaughter; they invade Judah to seize him; his own countrymen bind him and hand him over; and the Spirit rushes on him so that with a donkey's jawbone he kills a thousand. Then, near death from thirst, he cries to the LORD and God gives water. One movement holds together human vengeance and divine deliverance.
section.thesis: Personal revenge spirals into open war, yet through Samson's flawed vengeance the LORD grants Israel a great deliverance, and the thirsty deliverer learns that his strength and his very life are gifts he must ask of God.
section.frameNote: Five beats: the foxes and the burning fields, verses 1 to 5; blood for blood, verses 6 to 8; Judah binds its own deliverer, verses 9 to 13; the jawbone and the thousand, verses 14 to 17; and the cry for water at En-hakkore, verses 18 to 20.

units.label: The foxes and the burning fields

verses.text: Later on, at the time of the wheat harvest, Samson took a young goat and went to visit his wife. “I want to go to my wife in her room,” he said. But her father would not let him enter.
“I was sure that you thoroughly hated her,” said her father, “so I gave her to one of the men who accompanied you. Is not her younger sister more beautiful than she? Please take her instead.”
Samson said to them, “This time I will be blameless in doing harm to the Philistines.”
Then Samson went out and caught three hundred foxes. And he took torches, turned the foxes tail-to-tail, and fastened a torch between each pair of tails.
Then he lit the torches and released the foxes into the standing grain of the Philistines, burning up the piles of grain and the standing grain, as well as the vineyards and olive groves.

units.label: Blood for blood
units.note: I will not rest until I have taken vengeance

verses.text: “Who did this?” the Philistines demanded. “It was Samson, the son-in-law of the Timnite,” they were told. “For his wife was given to his companion.” So the Philistines went up and burned her and her father to death.
And Samson told them, “Because you have done this, I will not rest until I have taken {{naqam|vengeance}} upon you.”
And he struck them ruthlessly with a great slaughter, and then went down and stayed in the cave at the rock of Etam.

units.label: Judah binds its deliverer
units.note: do you not realize the Philistines rule over us

verses.text: Then the Philistines went up, camped in Judah, and deployed themselves near the town of Lehi.
“Why have you attacked us?” said the men of Judah. The Philistines replied, “We have come to arrest Samson and pay him back for what he has done to us.”

In response, three thousand men of Judah went to the cave at the rock of Etam, and they asked Samson, “Do you not realize that the Philistines rule over us? What have you done to us?” “I have done to them what they did to me,” he replied.
But they said to him, “We have come down to arrest you and hand you over to the Philistines.” Samson replied, “Swear to me that you will not kill me yourselves.”
“No,” they answered, “we will not kill you, but we will tie you up securely and hand you over to them.” So they bound him with two new ropes and led him up from the rock.

When Samson arrived in Lehi, the Philistines came out shouting against him. And the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD came mightily upon him. The ropes on his arms became like burnt flax, and the bonds broke loose from his hands.
He found the fresh {{lechi|jawbone}} of a donkey, reached out his hand and took it, and struck down a thousand men.
Then Samson said: “With the jawbone of a donkey I have piled them into heaps. With the jawbone of a donkey I have slain a thousand men.”
And when Samson had finished speaking, he cast the jawbone from his hand; and he named that place Ramath-lehi.

units.label: The cry for water at En-hakkore
units.note: must I now die of thirst

verses.text: And being very thirsty, Samson cried out to the LORD, “You have accomplished this great {{teshuah|deliverance}} through Your servant. Must I now die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?”

So God opened up the hollow place in Lehi, and water came out of it. When Samson drank, his strength returned, and he was revived. That is why he named it En-hakkore, and it remains in Lehi to this day.
And Samson judged Israel for twenty years in the days of the Philistines.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. As in chapter 14, the ruach of the LORD comes mightily on Samson, verse 14, snapping his ropes like burnt flax so that he strikes down a thousand men. The Spirit is the true source of the deliverance, working through a man who is acting largely out of personal revenge, so that the victory is God's gift rather than Samson's achievement.

naqam.gloss: Vengeance, revenge, requital. Samson's cry, I will not rest until I have taken naqam, verse 7, drives the whole chapter's escalating cycle of retaliation. In Scripture vengeance ultimately belongs to God, Deuteronomy 32:35; Samson takes it into his own hands, and though God uses the result to strike the Philistines, the personal, appetite-driven revenge is portrayed honestly as flawed.

lechi.gloss: Jawbone; also the place name Lehi. Samson kills a thousand with the fresh lechi of a donkey, an unclean and unlikely weapon, then names the place Ramath-lehi, the hill of the jawbone. The wordplay ties weapon and place together and underlines the theme that God saves by weak and improbable means, not by conventional strength or arms.

teshuah.gloss: Deliverance, salvation, victory. In verse 18 Samson himself names his feat God's teshuah, this great deliverance accomplished through Your servant. The word reframes the slaughter: it was not merely Samson's revenge but God's saving act for Israel, and Samson's admission that his strength and life depend wholly on the LORD who granted it.

basic.context: After the wedding disaster of chapter 14, Samson returns to claim his wife, only to learn her father has given her to another. Declaring himself blameless, he burns the Philistines' harvest by tying torches to three hundred foxes. The Philistines retaliate by burning his wife and her father to death, the very fate she had once feared. Samson strikes them in a great slaughter, then hides at the rock of Etam. The Philistines invade Judah to seize him, and three thousand men of Judah, resigned to Philistine rule, bind their own deliverer and hand him over. But the Spirit of the LORD rushes on him; he breaks free, seizes a donkey's jawbone, and kills a thousand men. Then, dying of thirst, he cries to the LORD, who splits open a spring, and Samson revives. He judges Israel twenty years.
basic.hermeneutics: This is honest history of a cycle of revenge, told without whitewashing. Samson's motives are largely personal, tit-for-tat retaliation, and the narrator does not commend the appetite for vengeance that drives him. Yet the same story shows the Spirit granting a real deliverance and God answering a desperate prayer with mercy. We must not read Samson's private revenge as a model, nor deny that God genuinely worked through it to rescue His people and to teach His servant dependence.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on four words. The ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes on Samson, verse 14, and is the true power behind the victory. Samson's cry for naqam, vengeance, verse 7, drives the escalating cycle, though vengeance belongs to God. He kills a thousand with a lechi, a jawbone, then names the place after it, showing God saving by weak means. And Samson finally calls the whole event God's teshuah, deliverance, admitting his strength was a gift.
basic.typology: Samson is a deliverer who saves Israel, is handed over to the enemy by his own people, yet triumphs, and in this he faintly foreshadows Christ, who was delivered up by His own nation but conquered. The improbable weapon and the water given in the place of death also point, however dimly, to a God who saves through weakness and gives living water to the dying.
basic.christ: Samson's own countrymen, resigned to their bondage, bind him and hand him to the enemy, yet through that handing over comes deliverance. So the Lord Jesus was delivered up by His own people to the powers of this world, yet through that very handing over He won salvation. Where Samson took vengeance and killed his enemies, Christ, the greater Deliverer, bore vengeance in Himself and prayed for His enemies, conquering not by slaughter but by His death. And the water God gave the dying Samson points to Christ, who gives living water so we never thirst.
basic.god: The chapter reveals a God who is sovereign over human vengeance, weaving even Samson's flawed retaliation into a real deliverance for His people. He empowers by His Spirit, He saves by unlikely means, a jawbone rather than an army, and He is tender to His exhausted servant, hearing his cry and opening a spring. God is shown to be both the true author of the deliverance and the merciful giver of life to the one who calls on Him.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 32 declares that vengeance belongs to God. Romans 12 tells believers never to avenge themselves but to leave room for God's wrath. John 4 and 7 present Christ as the giver of living water. And Judges 15:14 shows the Spirit rushing on the deliverer, the pattern of the judges.
basic.oneStory: In an age when Israel was content in its chains, God raised a flawed deliverer and worked through his very vengeance to strike the oppressor and rescue His people. The Spirit empowered him, unlikely means won the day, and a dying man was revived by God's mercy. It is one more step in the long story of God saving through weak instruments, pointing to Christ, the Deliverer handed over by His own, who conquers through death and gives living water.

context.literary: The chapter continues the Samson cycle with a tightly linked chain of cause and effect: each act of revenge provokes the next. The motif of fire runs through it, the burned harvest and the burned wife, and the improbable weapons, foxes and a jawbone, mark Samson as a wild, unconventional deliverer. The closing scene shifts abruptly from slaughter to a prayer of weakness, and the naming of Ramath-lehi and En-hakkore fixes the events in the land's memory.
context.historical: The wheat harvest was the Philistines' vital food supply, so burning the standing grain, vineyards, and olive groves struck at their economy. Judah, under Philistine domination, had grown so accustomed to servitude that they policed their own deliverer to keep the peace. Lehi lay in the Judean foothills; naming sites after events, as with Ramath-lehi and En-hakkore, was a common way to preserve their significance.
context.canonical: Samson stands late in the downward spiral of Judges, when even Israel's own tribe would rather hand over its deliverer than be free. This tragic passivity exposes the nation's need for a true King and Saviour. The Spirit-empowered deliverance and the answered prayer belong to the pattern of the judges, while the handing over of the deliverer by his own people anticipates, at a great distance, the betrayal and triumph of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God granting Israel a real deliverance through Samson's flawed vengeance, to expose Judah's shameful contentment in bondage, and to teach, through the cry at En-hakkore, that even the mighty deliverer depends wholly on the LORD for strength and life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Samson's escalating personal revenge; it does not prescribe it. His tit-for-tat retaliation is honestly portrayed, not held up as a pattern for God's people, whom Scripture commands to leave vengeance to God. What endures is God's sovereign use of the situation, His salvation by weak means, and His mercy to the one who calls on Him.

debates.question: How do God's sovereign deliverance and Samson's sinful vengeance relate in this chapter?

views.label: God overrules the vengeance without endorsing it
views.case: Scripture reserves vengeance for God, Deuteronomy 32:35, so Samson's personal retaliation is not commended. Yet the Spirit genuinely empowers him and God grants a real teshuah, deliverance, showing that the LORD can accomplish His saving purpose through flawed and even sinful human acts without approving the sin in them.

views.label: Samson acts, in part, as God's appointed agent of judgment
views.case: Because Samson was raised up specifically to strike the Philistines, some see his blows as a form of divinely sanctioned judgment on an oppressing enemy, so that his actions carry a genuine, if imperfect, judicial role in God's dealings with the Philistines.

debates.bearing: Holding the first as primary keeps us from baptizing personal revenge; God's use of Samson's deeds is providence, not permission. It assures us God can bring good even from tangled motives, while still calling us to leave vengeance to Him.

debates.question: What is the point of Samson's prayer and near-death from thirst at En-hakkore?

views.label: A lesson in the deliverer's dependence
views.case: Immediately after his greatest triumph, Samson is helpless with thirst and must cry to God, who alone gave the victory. The scene deflates any self-reliance: the same strength that killed a thousand cannot make water, teaching that Samson's power and very life are gifts from the LORD.

views.label: Evidence of real, if immature, faith
views.case: Samson rightly names the victory God's deliverance through His servant and calls on the LORD in his need; the answered prayer shows a genuine, though undeveloped, relationship with God beneath the bravado.

debates.bearing: Both readings humble and encourage: the strongest are still utterly dependent on God, and God graciously hears even a flawed servant's honest cry, giving water to the thirsty.

study.originalLanguages: Four words carry the chapter. The engine of the plot is naqam, vengeance: I will not rest until I have taken vengeance upon you, verse 7. In Scripture vengeance belongs to the LORD, Deuteronomy 32:35, and the narrator lets us feel how Samson's private, appetite-driven revenge sets off an escalating cycle of fire and blood, honestly shown as flawed even as God uses it. The turning point is the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, who comes mightily on Samson, verse 14, so that ropes fall away like burnt flax; the deliverance is plainly the Spirit's doing, not Samson's own. God's chosen weapon is a lechi, a donkey's jawbone, unclean and improbable, with which a thousand fall, and the place is named Ramath-lehi in memory; the LORD saves by weak and unlikely means. And at the end Samson himself renames the whole affair: it was God's teshuah, this great deliverance, accomplished through Your servant, verse 18. In one word the killing is reframed as salvation and Samson's boast collapses into dependence, for the man who slew a thousand cannot even make water and must cry to the God who gave him everything.

typology.type: The deliverer handed over to the enemy by his own people, yet triumphant
typology.fulfillment: Christ, delivered up by His own nation to the powers, yet conquering through that very handing over, Acts 2:23 and Colossians 2:15.

typology.type: Deliverance won by a weak and improbable weapon
typology.fulfillment: God saving through the weakness of the cross, the power and wisdom of God, 1 Corinthians 1:25-27.

typology.type: Water given to the dying at En-hakkore, the spring of him who calls
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who gives living water so that whoever drinks will never thirst, John 4:14 and 7:37-38.

study.christ: Samson's own tribe, resigned to their chains, bind their deliverer and hand him to the enemy; yet through that handing over God works Israel's rescue. In this Samson faintly reflects the Lord Jesus, who was delivered up by His own people to the powers of this world, Acts 2:23, and through that very betrayal accomplished salvation. But the contrast is sharp. Samson takes vengeance and slaughters his enemies; Christ, the greater Deliverer, absorbs vengeance in Himself and prays for those who kill Him, Luke 23:34, conquering not by the sword but by His cross. Samson is revived by water God gives the dying; Christ is Himself the living water, given so that we who are dying of thirst may drink and live, John 7:37-38. Where Samson calls, the spring of him who calls opens; and everyone who calls on the name of the Lord Jesus is saved.
study.god: This chapter shows a God who is sovereign over even human vengeance, weaving Samson's flawed retaliation into a genuine deliverance for a people too passive to seek it. He empowers by His Spirit, snapping ropes like burnt flax, and He saves by unlikely means, a jawbone rather than an army, so that no one can boast. And He is tender toward His exhausted servant: when the mighty man collapses in thirst and cries out, God does not despise the prayer but splits open a spring and revives him. The LORD is at once the true author of the victory, the judge of the oppressing Philistines, and the merciful giver of life to the one who calls on Him, so that all the glory of the deliverance belongs to Him.

crossReferences.note: Vengeance is Mine, says the LORD; the standard against which Samson's personal revenge is measured.
Never avenge yourselves, but leave room for God's wrath, the New Testament application for God's people.
God chose the weak and foolish things to shame the strong, as He saves by a jawbone and a spring.
Christ invites the thirsty to come and drink, the living water to which En-hakkore points.

study.oneStory: In an age when Israel was so at home in its chains that it would bind its own deliverer, God worked through Samson's very vengeance to strike the oppressor and rescue His people. The Spirit empowered him, an improbable weapon won the day, and a dying man was revived by God's mercy at the spring of him who calls. It is one more step in the long account of a God who saves through weak and unworthy instruments, pointing beyond Samson to Christ, the Deliverer handed over by His own, who conquers through death and gives the living water that ends our thirst forever.

quiz.q: What set off the chain of revenge in this chapter?

options.text: Samson's wife had been given to another man, so he burned the Philistines' harvest, and the cycle escalated from there.
options.note: Verses 1-5 show the lost wife leading Samson to burn the fields, igniting the retaliation.

options.text: The Philistines attacked Samson's home without any provocation.
options.note: There was conflict, but the chapter traces the spiral back to the lost wife and the burned fields.

options.text: The men of Judah started a war against the Philistines.
options.note: Judah did the opposite; they tried to hand Samson over to keep the peace.

options.text: God commanded Samson to take personal revenge.
options.note: Scripture reserves vengeance for God; Samson acted on his own grievance.

quiz.q: How did the men of Judah respond to the Philistine threat?

options.text: They bound their own deliverer and handed him over, resigned to Philistine rule.
options.note: Verses 11-13 show Judah's shameful passivity, binding Samson to keep the peace.

options.text: They rallied behind Samson to fight for their freedom.
options.note: The opposite: they were content in bondage and delivered Samson to the enemy.

options.text: They asked Samson to lead them into battle.
options.note: They came not to enlist him but to arrest him and hand him over.

options.text: They fled the region entirely.
options.note: They stayed and negotiated the surrender of Samson, not flight.

quiz.q: With what did Samson strike down a thousand Philistines?

options.text: The fresh jawbone of a donkey, after the Spirit of the LORD rushed on him.
options.note: Verses 14-15 credit the Spirit and the improbable weapon of a donkey's jawbone.

options.text: A sword he took from a fallen Philistine.
options.note: He used a jawbone, not a sword; the weakness of the weapon is the point.

options.text: His bare hands alone.
options.note: He broke his ropes by the Spirit, but he took up a jawbone to strike them down.

options.text: The help of the three thousand men of Judah.
options.note: Judah had bound him; he fought alone as the Spirit empowered him.

quiz.q: What did Samson do when he was dying of thirst after the battle?

options.text: He cried out to the LORD, naming the victory God's deliverance, and God opened a spring to revive him.
options.note: Verses 18-19 show his prayer of dependence and God's merciful provision of water.

options.text: He found a well and drew water by his own strength.
options.note: He had no strength left for that; God had to open the spring in answer to prayer.

options.text: He demanded that Judah bring him water.
options.note: He turned to God in prayer, not to the men who had bound him.

options.text: He gave up and prepared to die.
options.note: He did not give up; he called on the LORD, who answered.

quiz.q: How does Samson in this chapter point us to Christ?

options.text: He is handed over by his own people yet brings deliverance, foreshadowing Christ, though Christ conquers by bearing vengeance rather than taking it.
options.note: Samson points to Christ by likeness and contrast; Christ saves through His death, not slaughter.

options.text: He shows that taking personal revenge is the way to serve God.
options.note: His Spirit-empowered deliverance is real, but his personal vengeance is a warning, not a model.

options.text: He proves God only saves through strong and impressive means.
options.note: The opposite: a jawbone and a spring show God saving by weakness.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ whatsoever.
options.note: As a deliverer handed over by his own who yet brings rescue, he clearly foreshadows Christ.

questions: The whole chapter is a spiral of revenge, each blow provoking the next. Where is a desire to get even quietly taking root in you, and what would it mean to leave vengeance to God?
Judah was so at home in bondage that they bound their own deliverer. Are there chains you have made peace with rather than longing to be free?
God won a great deliverance through an unclean, improbable jawbone. How does it encourage you that God delights to save through weak and unlikely means, including you?
At his strongest moment Samson was helpless with thirst and had to cry to God. Where do you need to admit that your gifts and successes still leave you utterly dependent on the Lord?
En-hakkore means the spring of him who calls, and Christ gives living water to the thirsty. What thirst are you carrying to Him this week to drink and be revived?

translationNotes.note: Struck them ruthlessly renders a Hebrew idiom the BSB footnote gives as literally he struck them hip and thigh, a vivid phrase for a thorough, devastating slaughter.
Deployed themselves near Lehi carries a BSB footnote reading literally and spread out in Lehi; Lehi means jawbone, anticipating the weapon and the naming of Ramath-lehi.
I have piled them into heaps involves a Hebrew wordplay on donkey and heap; the BSB footnote notes the line can also read I have made them into donkeys.
En-hakkore, as the BSB footnote explains, means the spring of him who calls, memorializing Samson's answered cry to the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 15:1-20. Poetic jawbone lines joined inline with surrounding prose. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon set to none; no verified expository sermon on this passage confirmed this session.
